name: Curley Russell
abstract: Dillon "Curley" Russell (March 19, 1917 – July 3, 1986) was an American jazz musician, who played bass on many bebop recordings.

works.t: I Don't Stand a Ghost of a Chance With You

about_quotes.speaker: Trummy Young
about_quotes.evidence: I had Dexter Gordon, Curly Russell and Denzil Best on drums... myself, and Curly Russell was on bass

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: Curly Russell is mentioned only as a member of the band ('Curly Russell is plucking', 'Curly Russell was on bass') during Jon Hendricks' brief encounter with Charlie Parker; no direct interaction or personal relationship between Hendricks and Russell is described.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: When we had the group together, sometimes we went out as the Messengers and the All- Star Jazz Messengers... His current group would open up. Then the old Messengers would come out and play.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: They weren’t people that I’d met, like . . . thinking of specific people that worked with Bud [Powell], like Curley Russell or somebody like that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: Alfred said, 'Well, Art is going to be on the date, and Curley Russell,' which was all right with me, because they could play.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sheila Jordan
about_quotes.evidence: I think he had... the first time I heard Bird, I think he had Max and, and he had Duke Jordan, and he had Curly Russell
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheila Jordan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
